note: Local Sandy / Salt Lake City Medicare blog. Long-form posts per the Ambrose medicare-blog-writer skill; figures are CY2026, verified via the Ambrose Brain (CMS Medicare Plan Landscape) or CMS.gov. Daily posts are prepended by engine/add-post.js. Newest first.

posts.title: Choosing a Medicare Advantage Plan in Salt Lake County: The Complete 2026 Guide
posts.excerpt: Salt Lake County has 41 Medicare Advantage plans for 2026 — 17 of the 24 MA-PD plans at $0 premium. A Sandy-based advisor's complete guide to choosing well.
tldr: Salt Lake County offers 41 Medicare Advantage plans for CY2026: 24 standard MA-PD plans plus 17 Special Needs Plans (SNPs), per the CMS Medicare Plan Landscape.
17 of the 24 MA-PD plans carry a $0 monthly premium — but you still pay your Part B premium ($202.90 standard in 2026) and copays as you use care.
In-network out-of-pocket maximums on 2026 Salt Lake County plans range from $3,500 to $9,250 — this number matters more than the premium.
The top overall CMS star rating in the county is 4.5 stars, and 11 of the 22 rated plans earn 4 stars or better.
The right plan is decided by your doctors, your prescriptions, and your pharmacy — not by the premium. Compare against your own list before you enroll.

body.h: How many Medicare Advantage plans are in Salt Lake County for 2026?
p: For the 2026 plan year, Salt Lake County residents can choose from 41 Medicare Advantage plans, according to the CMS Medicare Plan Landscape: 24 standard Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MA-PD) plans plus 17 Special Needs Plans (SNPs) for people with specific chronic conditions or dual Medicare/Medicaid eligibility. That's one of the deepest plan markets in Utah — deeper than Utah County (21 MA-PD plans), Weber County (19), or Grand County (8) for the same year.

Wide choice is genuinely good news: more carriers competing means more $0-premium options, richer extras, and more network combinations. But it also means the odds of picking a mediocre fit by guessing are high. Two plans that look identical on a postcard can treat your specific medications and your specific doctors completely differently.

body.h: What do these plans actually cost?
p: Of the 24 standard MA-PD plans in Salt Lake County, 17 have a $0 monthly plan premium for 2026, and premiums top out at $135 per month. A $0 premium does not mean free healthcare. Everyone with Medicare still pays the Part B premium — $202.90 per month standard in 2026 — and you'll pay copays and coinsurance as you actually use services: a primary care visit, a specialist, an MRI, a hospital stay.

What protects you from a catastrophic year is the plan's in-network maximum out-of-pocket (MOOP) limit. For 2026 Salt Lake County plans, MOOPs range from $3,500 to $9,250. Once your in-network spending hits that ceiling, the plan pays 100% of covered in-network care for the rest of the year. Two $0-premium plans can be $5,750 apart in worst-case exposure — which is why we compare MOOPs before premiums, every time.
list: 17 of 24 MA-PD plans: $0 plan premium (2026)
Premium range across the county: $0–$135/month
Part B premium still applies: $202.90/mo standard (2026)
In-network out-of-pocket max: $3,500–$9,250 depending on plan (2026)
Top overall CMS star rating in the county: 4.5 stars

body.h: Star ratings: what 4.5 stars actually tells you
p: CMS rates Medicare Advantage plans from 1 to 5 stars each year based on dozens of quality measures — preventive care, chronic condition management, member complaints, customer service responsiveness, and drug safety among them. For 2026, the top overall rating available in Salt Lake County is 4.5 stars, and 11 of the 22 rated plans in the county earn 4 stars or higher.

Ratings matter for two practical reasons. First, they're a decent proxy for how much friction you'll experience — plans with chronically low ratings tend to generate more denials, more paperwork, and more phone calls. Second, if a 5-star plan is available in your county, Medicare allows you to switch into it once per year outside normal enrollment windows. Ratings shouldn't be the only filter, but we'd think hard before choosing a plan under 3.5 stars when 4+ star alternatives cover the same doctors.

body.h: The network question: will your doctors be covered?
p: This is the question that actually decides which plan is right, and it's the one most people skip. Medicare Advantage plans in Utah are built around provider networks — most commonly HMO, HMO-POS, or PPO structures — and along the Wasatch Front those networks are typically anchored by either Intermountain Health or University of Utah Health facilities and physician groups.

For Sandy-area residents, that means checking your specific hospital preference — Alta View, Intermountain Medical Center in Murray, St. Mark's, or University of Utah Health locations — plus your primary care physician and every specialist you see. An HMO plan generally requires you to stay in-network except for emergencies; a PPO lets you go out-of-network at a higher cost. Before any client enrolls, we run every provider on their list against the plan's current directory, because 'my doctor probably takes it' is how people end up with a plan they resent by February.

body.h: The drug list: same medications, very different bills
p: Every MA-PD plan has its own formulary — the list of drugs it covers, the tier each drug sits on, and which pharmacies get preferred pricing. Two neighbors taking the same five medications can pay hundreds of dollars a year apart purely because of which plan each one picked.

The good news for 2026: your out-of-pocket spending on covered Part D drugs is capped at $2,100 for the year. Hit that number and you pay $0 for covered drugs the rest of the year. But the cap doesn't make plan choice irrelevant — the plan still decides how fast and how painfully you get there, through tiers, deductibles (up to $615 standard in 2026, though many Salt Lake County plans reduce or waive it on lower tiers), and pharmacy pricing. We match your exact medication list, doses included, against each candidate plan's formulary before recommending anything.

body.h: Extras: dental, vision, hearing — worth weighing, not leading with
p: Most Salt Lake County Advantage plans bundle benefits Original Medicare doesn't cover: dental cleanings and dentures, vision exams and eyewear allowances, hearing aids, fitness memberships, and over-the-counter allowances. These are real money — a decent hearing aid benefit alone can be worth thousands.

Our advice is to treat extras as the tiebreaker, not the headline. A generous dental allowance doesn't compensate for a plan that puts your cardiologist out-of-network or your insulin on a high tier. Get the doctors and drugs right first; then let the extras break the tie between finalists.

body.h: What about Special Needs Plans (SNPs)?
p: Seventeen of Salt Lake County's 41 plans are Special Needs Plans — plans designed for specific situations. Chronic-condition SNPs (C-SNPs) serve people with conditions like diabetes, cardiovascular disorders, or chronic heart failure; Dual-eligible SNPs (D-SNPs) serve people who qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid. These plans tailor their benefits, provider networks, and drug formularies to their population, and often include enhanced care coordination.

If you have a qualifying chronic condition or Medicaid eligibility, a SNP can be meaningfully better than a general plan — but eligibility is verified, and the fit still depends on networks and formularies. It's worth a conversation rather than a guess.

body.h: When you can enroll or switch
p: The Annual Enrollment Period (AEP) runs October 15 through December 7 every year — that's when anyone on Medicare can join, drop, or switch Medicare Advantage and Part D plans for the following January 1. If you're already in an Advantage plan, the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 – March 31) gives you one additional switch — to a different Advantage plan or back to Original Medicare with a drug plan.

Outside those windows, Special Enrollment Periods open when life changes: moving out of your plan's service area, losing employer coverage, gaining Medicaid or Extra Help eligibility, or your plan leaving the county. And if you're new to Medicare, your 7-month Initial Enrollment Period around your 65th birthday is your first chance to choose.

body.h: A simple, honest way to choose
p: List your doctors and preferred hospital. List every prescription with its dose. Name the pharmacy you actually use. Then compare plans in this order: network first, drug coverage second, out-of-pocket maximum third, extras fourth, premium last. It's the reverse of how plans are advertised, and it's the order that predicts whether you'll be happy in July, not just in January.

As an independent, Sandy-based agency near 8915 South 700 East, we run this comparison with clients at no cost — we're paid by carriers when you enroll, so our help costs you the same as going direct. We do not offer every plan available in your area, and we review your plan every fall so a formulary or network change never blindsides you.

faq.q: How many Medicare Advantage plans are available in Salt Lake County in 2026?
faq.a: 41 plans — 24 standard MA-PD plans and 17 Special Needs Plans — according to the CMS Medicare Plan Landscape for CY2026.

faq.q: Are there $0-premium Medicare Advantage plans in Sandy, Utah?
faq.a: Yes. 17 of the 24 Salt Lake County MA-PD plans have a $0 monthly plan premium for 2026. You still pay the Part B premium ($202.90 standard in 2026) plus plan copays.

faq.q: Does a $0-premium plan mean free healthcare?
faq.a: No. You pay the Part B premium, plus copays and coinsurance up to the plan's out-of-pocket maximum — which ranges from $3,500 to $9,250 across 2026 Salt Lake County plans.

faq.q: What's the highest-rated Medicare Advantage plan in Salt Lake County?
faq.a: The top overall CMS star rating available in the county for 2026 is 4.5 stars, and 11 of the 22 rated plans earn 4 stars or better. Ratings are one input — network and drug fit still come first.

faq.q: Do Salt Lake County Advantage plans cover Intermountain and University of Utah doctors?
faq.a: Networks vary by plan — many are anchored by one system or the other. Before enrolling, verify your specific doctors and hospital against the plan's current directory rather than assuming.

faq.q: Can I switch plans if I choose wrong?
faq.a: Yes — during the fall Annual Enrollment Period (Oct 15 – Dec 7) for the following year, or during the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (Jan 1 – Mar 31) if you're already in an Advantage plan.

sources.name: CMS Medicare Plan Landscape CY2026 (via Ambrose Brain)
Medicare.gov — How Medicare Advantage star ratings work

posts.title: Medicare in Sandy, Utah for 2026: Every Change That Matters and What to Do About It
posts.excerpt: The 2026 Part D cap rises to $2,100, Part B hits $202.90, and Salt Lake County's plan lineup shifts. What Sandy seniors should do before fall enrollment.
tldr: The Part D out-of-pocket cap rises to $2,100 for 2026 (it was $2,000 in 2025) — after that, covered drugs cost you $0 for the rest of the year.
The standard Part B premium is $202.90/month in 2026, with a $283 annual deductible; the Part A hospital deductible is $1,736 per benefit period.
Salt Lake County's 2026 lineup: 41 Medicare Advantage plans, 17 of 24 MA-PD plans at $0 premium, top rating 4.5 stars.
The standard maximum Part D deductible rises to $615 in 2026 — many plans charge less, and formularies shift every year.
Your move: review your plan during AEP (October 15 – December 7) against next year's actual numbers, not this year's assumptions.

body.h: The headline change: a $2,100 cap on your drug costs
p: The biggest 2026 number for Sandy and Salt Lake City seniors is the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket cap: once your spending on covered prescription drugs reaches $2,100 for the year, you pay $0 for covered drugs through December 31. The cap — created by the Inflation Reduction Act — replaced the old 'donut hole' coverage gap entirely, and it adjusts annually (it was $2,000 in 2025).

For anyone on expensive brand-name drugs, insulin, or a long medication list, this is real protection: your worst-case pharmacy year now has a hard ceiling. But note what the cap doesn't do — it doesn't standardize how fast you reach it. Plans still differ on deductibles, tiers, and preferred pharmacies, so the months before you hit $2,100 can cost very different amounts depending on which plan you chose.

body.h: Spread the cost: the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan
p: Alongside the cap, every Part D plan must offer the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan — an option that spreads your out-of-pocket drug costs across the calendar year in monthly installments instead of charging you at the pharmacy counter. It's free to opt into, it doesn't reduce what you owe, and it's most useful for people whose drug costs are front-loaded — say, an expensive fill every month starting in January.

If January and February pharmacy bills have ever forced hard choices, ask your plan (or us) about enrolling in the payment option for 2026. It converts a $600 January into roughly equal monthly payments you can budget around.

body.h: What Original Medicare costs in 2026
p: Most people get Part A (hospital insurance) premium-free because they paid Medicare taxes during their working years — but Part A still carries a $1,736 deductible per benefit period in 2026. A benefit period isn't a calendar year: it starts when you're admitted and ends after 60 days without inpatient care, so it's possible to pay it more than once in a year.

Part B (medical insurance) has a standard premium of $202.90 per month in 2026 with a $283 annual deductible. Higher earners pay more through IRMAA — an income-based surcharge Social Security calculates from your tax return two years back, so 2026 surcharges are based on 2024 income. If your income has dropped since then because of retirement or another life change, you can ask Social Security to use your current income instead by filing Form SSA-44.
list: Part D out-of-pocket cap: $2,100 (2026)
Part D standard maximum deductible: $615 (2026)
Part B standard premium: $202.90/mo (2026)
Part B annual deductible: $283 (2026)
Part A hospital deductible: $1,736 per benefit period (2026)

body.h: Salt Lake County's 2026 plan lineup
p: Every fall, carriers reset premiums, formularies, provider networks, and extra benefits for the coming year — and the county lineup itself shifts as plans enter and exit. For 2026, Salt Lake County offers 41 Medicare Advantage plans (24 standard MA-PD plus 17 Special Needs Plans), with 17 of the 24 MA-PD plans at a $0 premium and a top overall rating of 4.5 stars, per the CMS Medicare Plan Landscape.

Around the Wasatch Front the numbers vary more than people expect: Utah County has 21 MA-PD plans (14 at $0), Davis County has 24 (17 at $0), and Weber County has 19 (13 at $0) for 2026. If you've moved — even one county over — your plan menu changed with you, and a Special Enrollment Period likely opened.

body.h: The change nobody announces: your own plan's fine print
p: The quiet risk each year isn't a new law — it's your current plan changing underneath you. Every September, plans mail an Annual Notice of Change (ANOC) listing next year's premium, deductible, copays, formulary moves, and network changes. Most people never read it. Then January arrives and a medication that cost $10 is suddenly on a higher tier, or a specialist quietly left the network.

Read the ANOC — or hand it to someone whose job is reading them. A ten-minute review in October routinely saves hundreds of dollars and a lot of frustration the following year. This is exactly what our free annual review is for: we check your actual doctors and prescriptions against your plan's actual 2026 terms, and only suggest a switch if it genuinely helps you.

body.h: Your 2026 action checklist
p: Here's what we'd tell a neighbor in Sandy to do this fall, in order:
list: Mark the Annual Enrollment Period: October 15 – December 7 (changes take effect January 1, 2026)
Read your plan's Annual Notice of Change when it arrives in September
Write down every prescription, dose, and your preferred pharmacy
List your doctors and preferred hospital (Intermountain, University of Utah Health, or others)
Compare your current plan against the 2026 lineup — or have an independent advisor run it free
If your income dropped since 2024, ask about Form SSA-44 to reduce an IRMAA surcharge
Ask about the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan if drug costs bunch up early in your year

body.h: The bottom line for Sandy seniors
p: 2026 is a genuinely better year to be a Medicare beneficiary than most: the $2,100 drug cap is the strongest cost protection Part D has ever had, and Salt Lake County's plan market is deep and competitive. The premiums tick up — that's real — but the bigger financial swing for most households is plan fit, not the standard premium.
Don't auto-renew blind. A short review against next year's real numbers is the cheapest insurance there is.

faq.q: What is the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket cap for 2026?
faq.a: $2,100. Once your out-of-pocket spending on covered Part D drugs reaches $2,100 in 2026, you pay nothing for covered drugs the rest of the year.

faq.q: How much is the Part B premium in 2026?
faq.a: The standard premium is $202.90 per month with a $283 annual deductible. Higher-income beneficiaries pay an IRMAA surcharge based on their 2024 tax return.

faq.q: When can I change my Medicare plan for 2026?
faq.a: The Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 – December 7, with changes effective January 1. Those already in a Medicare Advantage plan get one more switch during the January 1 – March 31 Open Enrollment Period.

faq.q: Did my Salt Lake County plan change for 2026?
faq.a: Almost certainly something changed — premiums, drug tiers, network, or extras. Your plan's Annual Notice of Change (mailed in September) lists everything; review it or have an advisor do it free.

faq.q: What is the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan?
faq.a: A no-cost option every Part D plan must offer that spreads your out-of-pocket drug costs into monthly installments across the year instead of paying at the pharmacy counter.

sources.name: CMS Medicare Plan Landscape CY2026 (via Ambrose Brain)

posts.title: Turning 65 in Salt Lake City? Your Complete 2026 Medicare Enrollment Timeline
posts.excerpt: A month-by-month Medicare enrollment roadmap for Salt Lake City and Sandy — your exact windows, the penalties you're avoiding, and a local checklist.
tldr: Your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) is a 7-month window: 3 months before your 65th birthday month, your birthday month, and 3 months after.
Enroll in the first 3 months of the window and coverage starts the month you turn 65 — no gap.

Delaying Part B without other creditable coverage adds 10% to your premium for every full 12 months delayed — for life. On 2026's $202.90 standard premium, one year's delay costs about $20.29/month forever.
The Part D late penalty is 1% of the national base premium ($38.99 in 2026) per month without creditable drug coverage — about 39 cents/month per month missed, permanently.
Your one-time Medigap Open Enrollment (6 months from your Part B effective date) is when supplements can't turn you down or charge more for health history.

body.h: The window that starts everything: your IEP
p: If you're turning 65 in Salt Lake City or Sandy, your first chance to enroll in Medicare is the Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) — a 7-month window built around your 65th birthday: the 3 months before your birthday month, your birthday month itself, and the 3 months after.

Timing inside the window matters. Enroll during the first three months and your coverage begins the first day of your birthday month, with no gap. Enroll during your birthday month or later and your start date slides — which can leave you weeks without coverage you thought you had. The clean move is simple: start the process about 90 days out.
list: Months 1–3 (before your birthday month): enroll now → coverage starts on time
Month 4 (your birthday month): coverage starts the following month
Months 5–7 (after): coverage start continues to slide — avoid if you can
Born on the 1st of a month? Your whole window shifts a month earlier

body.h: Month-by-month: what to do when
p: Here's the timeline we walk Salt Lake City clients through:
list: 6 months out — Confirm how you'll get retiree health coverage; ask HR how your employer plan coordinates with Medicare
4 months out — Gather your doctor list, medication list (with doses), and preferred pharmacy
3 months out — Your IEP opens: enroll in Parts A & B through ssa.gov (or by phone/office); decide your path — Original Medicare + Medigap + Part D, or Medicare Advantage
2 months out — Compare specific plans against your doctor/drug lists; check networks (Intermountain, University of Utah Health) and formularies
1 month out — Enroll in your chosen plan(s); confirm ID cards and effective dates
Birthday month — Coverage begins; fill a prescription and book a visit to confirm everything works
Within 6 months of Part B — Your one-time Medigap Open Enrollment runs; if you want a supplement, this is the guaranteed-acceptance window

body.h: Still working at 65? Read this before you delay anything
p: Plenty of Utahns work past 65, and if you (or your spouse) have group health coverage from an employer with 20 or more employees, you can usually delay Part B — and Part D, if the drug coverage is creditable — without penalty, then enroll later through a Special Enrollment Period when the employment or coverage ends.

But the rules have teeth. Employers under 20 employees generally can't substitute for Medicare — Medicare becomes primary at 65 and skipping Part B can leave you functionally uninsured. COBRA and retiree coverage do not count as active employment coverage for delaying Part B. And Health Savings Account contributions must stop once any part of Medicare starts (Part A enrollment is backdated up to 6 months, which trips up HSA savers regularly). Before you delay, get your exact situation confirmed — a wrong assumption here is one of the most expensive mistakes in all of Medicare.

body.h: The penalties, with real math
p: The late-enrollment penalties aren't one-time fees — they're permanent surcharges, which is why timing deserves respect.

Part B: delay enrollment without creditable employer coverage and Medicare adds 10% to your premium for each full 12-month period you should have been enrolled. Against 2026's standard $202.90 premium, a one-year delay adds roughly $20.29 every month, for the rest of your life. A three-year delay adds about $60.87 a month — over $730 a year, forever.

Part D: go 63 or more days without creditable drug coverage after your IEP and the penalty is 1% of the national base beneficiary premium ($38.99 in 2026) for every month you went without — added to your drug plan premium for as long as you have Part D. Fifteen uncovered months ≈ $5.85 added per month. The fix is cheap: even a low-premium drug plan keeps you penalty-free while your needs are simple.

body.h: Your fork in the road: two ways to get Medicare
p: During your IEP you'll choose between two paths, and it's easier when you know what each optimizes for.

Original Medicare + Medigap + Part D optimizes for freedom and predictability: see any doctor in the country that accepts Medicare, no referrals, very stable out-of-pocket costs — in exchange for a monthly Medigap premium. Medicare Advantage (Part C) optimizes for low premiums and bundled extras: 17 of Salt Lake County's 24 MA-PD plans are $0-premium for 2026, most include drug coverage plus dental/vision/hearing — in exchange for using a network and paying copays up to an out-of-pocket maximum ($3,500–$9,250 locally in 2026).

Neither is 'best' — CMS forbids that word for good reason. Travelers and snowbirds often lean Medigap; people who get care locally and want the lowest monthly outlay often lean Advantage. Your health, budget, and doctors decide.

body.h: The window people regret missing: Medigap Open Enrollment
p: If the supplement path appeals to you, one deadline outranks the rest: your Medigap Open Enrollment Period — the 6 months starting the month you're 65+ and enrolled in Part B. During this one-time window, insurers must accept you and cannot charge more based on your health history.

After it closes, most Medigap applications in Utah go through medical underwriting — carriers can decline you or price you up for pre-existing conditions. People who choose Advantage at 65 and later want to switch to a supplement sometimes discover the door is no longer wide open. That doesn't make Advantage wrong; it means the Medigap decision deserves a deliberate yes-or-no during your first six months, not a shrug.

body.h: Your Salt Lake City checklist
p: Turning 65 along the Wasatch Front? This is the short list we'd hand you:
list: Confirm your IEP dates (use our free Eligibility & IEP checker in the Tools section)
Decide whether you're delaying (only with qualifying employer coverage — verify, don't assume)
Gather doctors, prescriptions with doses, and your pharmacy
Choose your path: Original + Medigap + Part D, or Medicare Advantage
Verify your Intermountain / University of Utah Health providers against any plan you're considering
Enroll early in the window so coverage starts the month you turn 65
Calendar your Medigap Open Enrollment deadline — 6 months from your Part B effective date

faq.q: When should I sign up for Medicare if I'm turning 65?
faq.a: During your 7-month Initial Enrollment Period — ideally in the 3 months before your birthday month, so coverage starts the month you turn 65 with no gap.

faq.q: Can I delay Medicare if I'm still working at 65?
faq.a: Often yes — if you have creditable group coverage from an employer with 20+ employees, you can delay Part B penalty-free and enroll later via a Special Enrollment Period. Under-20-employee coverage, COBRA, and retiree plans generally don't qualify. Verify before delaying.

faq.q: How big is the Part B late-enrollment penalty?
faq.a: 10% of the premium for each full 12 months you delayed without creditable coverage — permanently. Against 2026's $202.90 standard premium, each year of delay costs about $20.29 more per month for life.

faq.q: How is the Part D late penalty calculated?
faq.a: 1% of the national base beneficiary premium ($38.99 in 2026) multiplied by your months without creditable drug coverage, rounded to the nearest 10 cents, added to your premium for as long as you have Part D.

faq.q: What is Medigap Open Enrollment and why does it matter?
faq.a: It's the 6-month window starting when you're 65+ with Part B, during which Medigap insurers must accept you regardless of health history. After it closes, most applications face medical underwriting in Utah.

faq.q: Should I pick Medicare Advantage or Medigap at 65?
faq.a: It depends on how you get care. Advantage offers low premiums and extras with networks and copays; Medigap costs more monthly but gives nationwide doctor freedom and predictable bills. An advisor can map your doctors, drugs, and budget to the better fit — free.
